title: The First Day of Thai Is the Tamil New Year Festival!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; the Thiruvalluvar-year formulation, `1971` Government-diary date and damaged classical quotation have been checked against the printed pages; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: At the DMK General Council held in Kalaignar Arangam at Anna Arivalayam, Chennai, on 9 January 2015, a special resolution congratulated the Professor, the Treasurer and me on being elected unanimously. One resolution condemned the Union Government and another the State Government. You would also have seen a third resolution published in the newspapers: Tamils should celebrate Pongal as the Tamil New Year with joy in every heart and an awakening of identity in every home.

On 23 January 2008, during the DMK Government, my dear friend Governor Surjit Singh Barnala addressed the Tamil Nadu Assembly. He explained that Tamil scholars, led by Maraimalai Adigal, the great scholar and founder of the Pure Tamil Movement, had decided that Tamils needed a distinct continuous calendar. They resolved to follow a continuous year in the name of Ayyan Thiruvalluvar, call it the “Tamil Year,” and place Thiruvalluvar's birth in 31 BCE.

The Governor said Chief Minister Kalaignar had accepted this principle thirty-seven years earlier and ordered its use in the Tamil Nadu Government diary from 1971 and the Government Gazette from 1972. Since Tamil scholars as a whole agreed that the Thiruvalluvar year and Tamil New Year begin on the first day of Thai, the Government had decided to declare and implement that day as the beginning of the Tamil New Year.

The people of Tamil Nadu, who already celebrated Pongal as the Tamil festival, would now celebrate it with double joy as Tamil New Year's Day. They would plant banana, mango and jackfruit trees; draw colourful *kolams*; make homes newly radiant with rows of lamps; wear new clothes; sing and dance in praise of Tamil honour and self-respect; spread equality; and pour out love in the bounding happiness that this was their Tamil New Year.

At the meeting chaired by Maraimalai Adigal, eminent scholars including Tamil Thendral Thiru. Vi. Kalyanasundaranar, Tamil guardian K. Subramania Pillai, Saivite elder Sachidananda Pillai, Navalar N.M. Venkatasamy Nattar, Navalar Somasundara Bharathiar and Muthamizh guardian K.A.P. Viswanatham resolved:
1. To follow a continuous calendar in Thiruvalluvar's name;
2. To celebrate it as the Tamil Year;
3. To obtain the Thiruvalluvar year by adding thirty-one to the Gregorian year in common use.

In 1939, the All India Tamil Conference met in Tiruchi under Navalar Somasundara Bharathiar. Thanthai Periyar, Karanthai Tamil Sangam president Uma Maheswaranar, Professor K. Subramaniam, T.P. Meenakshisundaranar, Thiru. Vi. Ka., Maraimalai Adigal, P.T. Rajan, Arcot Ramasamy Mudaliar, Revolutionary Poet Bharathidasan, Pattukkottai Alagiri and many others participated. That conference too declared the first day of Thai the Tamil New Year and Pongal the Tamil festival.

Responding to the DMK Government's announcement, Dravidar Kazhagam president and Tamil leader younger brother Veeramani said: “Our long-standing demand that the first day of Thai begin the Tamil New Year has succeeded. This is a new chapter of renaissance in the history of Tamil culture. We praise it and rejoice.”

On 24 January 2008, the day after the Governor's announcement, Kundrakudi Ponnambala Adigalar stated: “From ancient times, the first day of Thai has been a festival bringing awakening and joy to Tamil life through religion, life with nature, land and people, and agricultural life. Our thanks and praise go to Muthamizh scholar and Chief Minister Kalaignar for implementing the declaration by senior, incomparable Tamil scholars such as Maraimalai Adigal that the Tamil New Year in Ayyan Thiruvalluvar's name should begin on the first day of Thai.”

Poet Vairamuthu wrote: “Tamils of feeling celebrate and dance upon seeing the Kalaignar Government announce in the Governor's Address that the first day of Thai begins the Tamil New Year. Every national people has indestructible signs of identity. Tamils have an identity of land and an identity of race, but their identity of time alone remained confused. That darkness of confusion has broken and light has dawned today. The Dravidian movement presented Ayyan Thiruvalluvar as the measure of the world of ideas. Today, Kalaignar's Government has also made him the measure of time. This is history that corrects history.”

Tamils in Malaysia now celebrate the first day of Thai as New Year. Dr Mu. Varadarasanar explained: “In earlier times, the year did not begin on the first day of Chithirai. Elders celebrated the first day of Thai as New Year's Day. They bought and wore new clothes. They renewed the streets with fresh earth and red soil. Festivals were held throughout the town. In this way, cities celebrated Pongal as the birth of the New Year.”

Governor Barnala's announcement was regarded as one that gave all true Tamils great joy and pride. That year, Tamil people celebrated Pongal as Tamil New Year with unprecedented happiness. AIADMK leader Jayalalithaa could not tolerate it!

No sooner had the AIADMK returned to office in 2011 than she hurried to close down this declaration of Tamil identity, just as she tried to hold closing ceremonies for every good scheme introduced by the DMK Government.

Speaking in the Assembly on 23 August 2011, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said: “There is no basis for beginning the Tamil New Year in Thai, whereas much evidence supports beginning it in Chithirai. The law Karunanidhi introduced for self-promotion, hurting public sentiment, has been repealed.”

The AIADMK Government's act was like mixing poison into and destroying a good initiative enacted under the DMK. Tamil Nadu's history has always contained a deeply reactionary group that is hostile to Tamil itself and hates, excludes and neglects even the idea of Classical Tamil. One or two poets who hated Tamil existed in the past too. I explained this through a story at the time.

During Nakkiran's age, a man named Kuyakkondan reportedly said at a Tamil Sangam debate, “Aryan is good; Tamil is evil.” Nakkiran became angry and sang a curse: “You who called Tamil evil and the northern language good, die!” Kuyakkondan fell and died. Those present asked Nakkiran to restore him to life. Nakkiran then sang:
“For declaring Aryan good and Tamil evil,
He fell into Death's grip;
By the command of illustrious Agastya of Potiyil,
May pure Tamil restore him, *svaha*!”
Kuyakkondan reportedly rose alive.

When the Government announcement was made, the eminent Tamil epigraphist and scholar Iravatham Mahadevan asked: “If we lack the courage to examine and correct today's almanacs scientifically in light of current astronomy and the cycle of seasons, what is wrong in celebrating Pongal, the harvest festival, as New Year?”

Following the announcement in the Governor's Address, I introduced the Tamil Nadu Tamil New Year Bill, 2008, in the Assembly on 29 January. During debate on 1 February, E.S.S. Raman for the Congress, K. Arumugam for the PMK, N. Nanmaran for the CPI(M), V. Sivapunniyam for the CPI, M. Kannappan, then representing the MDMK, and K. Selvam for the Viduthalai Chiruthaigal welcomed it. The Bill passed.

Thus a praised and celebrated decision became law under the DMK and operated from 2008. Jayalalithaa changed it for the sole reason that it had been enacted under DMK rule. The BJP hurried to welcome her announcement. Journalists told me that both Communist parties opposed Jayalalithaa's Bill in the Assembly and asked for my view. I replied, “I bow to the Communists' Tamil feeling.”

PMK founder Dr Ramadoss said the AIADMK Government's decision would distress those with Tamil feeling, that abruptly moving Tamil New Year back to Chithirai was improper and would become a great historical error. Tamil leader K. Veeramani also strongly condemned it. I thanked them then for their Tamil feeling.
Revolutionary Poet Bharathidasan himself sang:
“Tamil asleep in slumber!
Chithirai is not your Tamil New Year.
The Aryan crowd that came to live by dependence
Gave you sixty years that defy reason.
For the Tamil who ruled the earth,
The first day of Thai is Tamil New Year!”
I wish to recall that poem too.

Today's rulers strain to conceal this historical truth and have ordered that Chithirai once again begin the Tamil year. The people of Tamil Nadu do not appear to have given their action any respect. Tamils living abroad understand the truth and celebrate the first day of Thai as the Tamil festival and the beginning of Tamil New Year.

Whether or not the AIADMK Government accepts it, let us not be concerned. I call upon all Tamils to come forward and celebrate the first day of Thai with distinction as the Tamil festival and the beginning of Tamil New Year. The first day of Thai is the Tamil New Year festival!
With affection,
M.K.
12 January 2015
